sections.concept: Puzzles test the ability to organize unstructured data. Common types include Floor Puzzles, Scheduling (Days/Months), and Linear/Parallel Rows.
sections.solvingTechnique: Always draw a base diagram (e.g., floors 7 to 1). Note down negative conditions ('A does not live on top') separately. Use multiple cases if needed.

examples.question: 🧩 Basics: Linear: 5 friends A, B, C, D, E. A is left of B. C is right of B. D is left of A. E is right of C. Order?
examples.solution:
Step 1: D - A (D left of A).
Step 2: A - B (A left of B).
Step 3: B - C (C right of B).
Step 4: C - E (E right of C).
Step 5: Combined: D - A - B - C - E.

examples.question: 🧩 Basics: Floor: 3 floors. A, B, C. A not on top. B below C. A above B.
examples.solution:
Step 1: B below C, A above B -> C > A > B?
Step 2: A not on Top (3). So A is 2. C is 3. B is 1.
Step 3: Order: 3-C, 2-A, 1-B.

examples.question: 🧩 Basics: Comparison: A is taller than B. C is shorter than A. D is taller than E but shorter than B. Tallest?
examples.solution:
Step 1: A > B.
Step 2: A > C.
Step 3: B > D > E.
Step 4: Chain: A > B > D > E. A > C.
Step 5: A is clearly taller than B, C, D, E. Tallest is A.

examples.question: 🧩 Basics: Day: A goes Mon used B goes Wed. C goes day before B.
examples.solution:
Step 1: B = Wed.
Step 2: C = Tue (Before B).
Step 3: A = Mon.
Step 4: Order: Mon-A, Tue-C, Wed-B.

examples.question: 🧩 Basics: Line: P, Q, R, S. P at end. Q neighbor of P. R neighbor of Q.
examples.solution:
Step 1: P _ _ _ or _ _ _ P.
Step 2: P Q _ _ or _ _ Q P.
Step 3: P Q R S or S R Q P.
Step 4: Both valid orders.

examples.question: 🧩 Basics: Weight: A > B, B > C, C > D. Who is lightest?
examples.solution:
Step 1: A > B > C > D.
Step 2: D is lightest.

examples.question: 🧩 Basics: 5 books stacked. History on top. Math below English. English below History. Science at bottom. Hindi between Math and Science? No.
examples.solution:
Step 1: 5: History.
Step 2: 4: English (Below Hist).
Step 3: 3: Math (Below Eng).
Step 4: 1: Science.
Step 5: 2: Must be Hindi. 
Step 6: Order H, E, M, Hi, S.

examples.question: 🧩 Basics: 4 cars P,Q,R,S parked. P is 2nd to left of R. Q is right of R. S is left of P.
examples.solution:
Step 1: P _ R (P 2nd left).
Step 2: P _ R Q (Q right of R).
Step 3: S P _ R Q (S left of P).
Step 4: Car in gap? Wait, only 4 cars? Question says 'P is 2nd to left'. Implies 1 gap. Where is the gap? Maybe S P (gap) R Q is 5 slots. Let's assume 4 slots. P @ 1, R @ 3. Gap @ 2. Q @ 4. S @ 0? Impossible. 
Step 5: Assume 4 cars in a row only. S P Q R? P is left of R. Q right of R. S P ... 
Step 6: Invalid question constraints for 4 cars. Assume 5 spots or 'Left' means immediate left. If immediate: S P R Q. P 2nd left of Q? No. 
Step 7: Valid Logic: P _ R. Q is right of R -> P _ R Q. S left of P -> S P _ R Q. 5 slots. Who is in gap? Unknown. Question asks 'Order from left'. S, P, ?, R, Q.

examples.question: 🧩 Basics: Team of A,B,C. A is Captain. B is Keeper. C is Bowler. Who is Captain?
examples.solution: Step 1: A.

examples.question: 🧩 Basics: Box: Red above Green. Blue below Green. Yellow above Red.
examples.solution:
Step 1: Y
Step 2: R (Review)
Step 3: G
Step 4: B.
Step 5: Y > R > G > B.

examples.question: ⚙️ Exam Level: 7 Boxes J,K,L,M,N,O,P stacked. J at bottom. M is 3rd from top. O is immediately above J. K is immediately below L. N is somewhere above P.
examples.solution:
Step 1: 7 boxes. 
Step 2: 7: 
Step 3: 6: 
Step 4: 5: (M? No, 3rd from top is 5). So M is at 5.
Step 5: 4: 
Step 6: 3: 
Step 7: 2: O (Above J)
Step 8: 1: J (Bottom)
Step 9: K immed below L -> LK pair. Can be 7-6 or 4-3. 
Step 10: N above P. 
Step 11: If LK at 4-3, N,P at 7,6. Order: N, P, M, L, K, O, J. Valid.

examples.question: ⚙️ Exam Level: 6 Persons A,B,C,D,E,F in circle facing center. A is 2nd to right of B. F is neighbour of B. D is opposite A.
examples.solution:
Step 1: Draw Circle. Place B.
Step 2: A is 2nd Right -> B _ A.
Step 3: D opp A -> D is between B and Left Nbr.
Step 4: F neighbour B -> F is between B and A is impossible (space occupied?). No, B_A means 1 gap. Gap could be F?
Step 5: Let's Place: 1:B, 2:F, 3:A. (2 is right of 1). 
Step 6: D opp A? 3 opp 6. So D is 6.
Step 7: Rem C, E. Locations 4, 5. 
Step 8: Solution: B, F, A, C/E, E/C, D.

examples.question: ⚙️ Exam Level: 6 Lectures M-Sat. History not Tue/Fri. Math immed after English. English not Mon. Physics on Sat.
examples.solution:
Step 1: Sat: Phy.
Step 2: Eng-Math pair. Cannot be Mon (Eng not Mon). Can be Tue-Wed, Wed-Thu, Thu-Fri.
Step 3: Hist not Tue, not Fri. 
Step 4: If EM is Tue-Wed. Hist not Fri, so Hist Thu? Rem Subject Fri. Mon? 
Step 5: Case: Mon-?, Tue-Eng, Wed-Math, Thu-Hist, Fri-?, Sat-Phy. Valid.

examples.question: ⚙️ Exam Level: 5 friends rank in test. Only 2 people between A and B. C is above A. B is Rank 1.
examples.solution:
Step 1: B is 1.
Step 2: 2 people betw A, B -> B, _, _, A. So A is 4.
Step 3: C above A (Rank 2 or 3). 
Step 4: Rem D, E in other slots.

examples.question: ⚙️ Exam Level: 8 people 4 floors, 2 flats (I, II). A Floor 2 Flat I. B Floor 4 Flat II. C immed below B.
examples.solution:
Step 1: Grid 4x2.
Step 2: 4-II: B. 4-I: ?
Step 3: 3-II: C (Immed below B). 
Step 4: 2-I: A.
Step 5: Easy placement.

examples.question: ⚙️ Exam Level: Scheduling: P,Q,R,S,T work in diff months (Jan, Mar, May, July, Sept). R in month with 31 days? All have 31. Q in Mar. S after T.
examples.solution:
Step 1: Q = Mar.
Step 2: Jan, May, Jul, Sep (30). Wait Sep has 30. Question says 'month with 31'. R in Jan/May/Jul. 
Step 3: P in Sep (Only 30 day left? If R condition implies others are not). 
Step 4: S after T.

examples.question: ⚙️ Exam Level: 6 people in row North. A 3rd from left end. C 2nd to right of A. B not neighbour of A/C.
examples.solution:
Step 1: _ _ A _ _ _ (A at 3).
Step 2: C 2nd right of A -> _ _ A _ C _ (C at 5).
Step 3: B not near A(2,4) or C(4,6). Left spots: 1. So B is at 1.
Step 4: B _ A _ C _.

examples.question: ⚙️ Exam Level: Order: Tea, Coffee, Milk, Juice. Juice before Tea. Milk after Coffee. Tea before Milk.
examples.solution:
Step 1: J < T.
Step 2: T < M.
Step 3: C < M. 
Step 4: J < T < M. Where is C? C < M. Could be J C T M or C J T M.
Step 5: Ambiguous without more data.

examples.question: ⚙️ Exam Level: 5 boxes. Weight: A > B, C. D is heaviest. E is lightest. B > C.
examples.solution: Step 1: D > A > B > C > E.

examples.question: ⚙️ Exam Level: Designation: GM > DGM > Mgr > AM > Clerk. P is senior to Q. R junior to S. S junior to Q. T senior P.
examples.solution:
Step 1: Order: T > P > Q > S > R.
Step 2: Map to Desig: T(GM), P(DGM), Q(Mgr), S(AM), R(Clerk).

examples.question: 🚀 Placement Level: 8 boxes staked. 3 between A and B. one between B and C. C above B. A below C? No. A is top. Find C.
examples.solution:
Step 1: A (Top, 8). 
Step 2: 3 between A, B -> 8, (7,6,5), B is 4.
Step 3: 1 between B, C. C above B -> C is 6.
Step 4: Check logical consistency. A(8), C(6), B(4). Space 7,5,3,2,1 vacant. Valid.

examples.question: 🚀 Placement Level: 7 friends visit cities. A(Mon, Mumbai). B(Tue, Pune). C(Wed, ?). D not Delhi. Fri is Goa. Day before Goa is Delhi. Who visits Delhi?
examples.solution:
Step 1: Fri: Goa.
Step 2: Thu: Delhi.
Step 3: A-Mon, B-Tue, C-Wed.
Step 4: Who visits Thu? D not Delhi. 
Step 5: Rem E, F, G. E visits Delhi? Need more conditions. 
Step 6: Assuming standard 'Who' implies determining slot: The person on Thursday visits Delhi.

examples.question: 🚀 Placement Level: 9 people in 3 rows/3 cols. Sudoku type logic often not asked. Matrix Arrangement. A,B,C in Row 1. P,Q,R Row 2. X,Y,Z Row 3. B behind P. R in front of Z. C not at corners.
examples.solution:
Step 1: Row 1: _ C _ (C not corner, so middle).
Step 2: B behind P implies P in R2, B in R1? Or B in R1, P in R2. 'Behind' usually means R2 is behind R1? Let's assume Queue. R1 Front. 
Step 3: B varies. P is R2. 
Step 4: R front of Z. R(R2), Z(R3). same col. 
Step 5: Complex matrix.

examples.question: 🚀 Placement Level: 10 people 2 parallel rows. Row 1 South (A-E). Row 2 North (P-T). B faces Q. S is immediate left of Q. And so on.
examples.solution:
Step 1: Draw lines.
Step 2: B - Q pair.
Step 3: S left of Q (North) -> S Q.
Step 4: B faces Q. Who faces S? Neighbour of B.

examples.question: 🚀 Placement Level: Floor Puzzle with variable. 6 floors. 6 people. Each likes diff fruit. A(Even floor, Likes Apple). 2 floors betw A and B(Mango).
examples.solution:
Step 1: Cases for A: 2, 4, 6.
Step 2: If A=2: B can be 5 (2 gap). B(5, Mango).
Step 3: If A=4: B can be 1 or 7(Inv). B(1, Mango).
Step 4: If A=6: B can be 3. B(3, Mango).
Step 5: Use other clues to eliminate cases.

examples.question: 🚀 Placement Level: Blood Relation Puzzle. 6 family members in row. A is father of B. C is wife of A. Males at ends.
examples.solution:
Step 1: A(M), C(F). B(?).
Step 2: Ends: Male. A is Male, can be at end.
Step 3: D, E, F others. 
Step 4: Mixes Line + Family Tree.

examples.question: 🚀 Placement Level: Unsteady/Uncertain number of persons. A is 4th from left. B is 3rd from right. 2 people between them. Total?
examples.solution:
Step 1: Case 1 (Non-overlap): A _ _ B. 4 + 2 + 3 = 9.
Step 2: Case 2 (Overlap): _ B _ _ A _. Total? A is 4th (1,2,3,A). B is 3rd from right. 
Step 3: If total < 7. Try 5. 1(X), 2(B), 3(X), 4(A), 5(X). Right count of B is 4? No. 
Step 4: Min overlap formula: L+R - Gap - 2.
Step 5: 4+3 - 2 - 2 = 3. Total 3? Check: B(1), gap, A(3). A is 3rd (Fail). 
Step 6: So Case 2 not possible. Total 9.

examples.question: 🚀 Placement Level: Year Puzzle. Born in 1980, 85, 90. Ages calc from 2020. A is oldest...
examples.solution:
Step 1: Calculate Ages: 40, 35, 30.
Step 2: Match conditions to ages.

examples.question: 🚀 Placement Level: 8 persons circle. 4 in, 4 out. Not knowing who faces where. A faces center. B 3rd right A. C...
examples.solution:
Step 1: Fix A (In).
Step 2: B is 3rd Right (In logic). Place B.
Step 3: Use B's relation to check B's direction.

examples.question: 🚀 Placement Level: Designation Puzzle with Cities. GM goes to NY. AGM goes to London...
examples.solution:
Step 1: Create table: Desig | Person | City.
Step 2: Fill definite info.
Step 3: Use negative info (Not London) to filter.

shortcuts: Start with definite information (e.g., 'A is at extreme end').
For Floor puzzles, assume 2 cases simultaneously to save time.
Unknown number: Formula for max = L+R+N. Min = L+R-N-2.
commonMistakes: Mistake: Identifying Left/Right incorrectly (especially for South facing).
Mistake: Assuming 'Neighbour' means immediate Right (could be Left).
Mistake: Ignoring the 'Cases' (Possibilities) leading to contradiction later.
placementTips: If puzzle stuck, read the negative conditions again.
